sources.name: Strunk & White, Elements of Style — Rule 16: Keep Related Words Together
sources.note: Public domain. Subject and principal verb should not be separated by a phrase or clause that can be transferred elsewhere.

sources.name: Strunk & White, Elements of Style — Rule 10: Use the Active Voice
sources.note: Strong transitive verbs over forms of to be. Verb carries the action.

sources.name: Purdue OWL — Making Subjects and Verbs Agree
sources.note: Subject-verb proximity and agreement as clarity foundation.

_description: The verb must follow the subject closely. Strong transitive verbs are preferred over forms of to be. The verb carries the action — it is not a placeholder or filler.

rules.rule: The verb must follow the subject within 5 words.
rules.rationale: Subject-verb proximity is the primary driver of sentence clarity. When more than 5 words separate subject from verb, the reader loses the thread of the sentence.
rules.example_correct: The guard closed the gate at midnight.
rules.example_incorrect: The guard, after receiving orders from the shift supervisor late in the evening, closed the gate.

rules.rule: Do not separate subject and verb with a long intervening clause.
rules.rationale: An intervening clause between subject and verb forces the reader to hold the subject in memory while processing unrelated information. Move the clause to the beginning or end of the sentence.
rules.example_correct: After receiving orders from the supervisor, the guard closed the gate.
rules.example_incorrect: The guard, after receiving orders from the supervisor, closed the gate at midnight without incident.
rules.fix: Move the intervening clause to the front or rear of the sentence.

rules.rule: Prefer strong transitive verbs over forms of to be.
rules.rationale: Forms of to be — is, are, was, were, been, being — are placeholders. They carry no action. Replace with a verb that describes what the subject actually does.
rules.example_incorrect: Three bridges were affected by the flood.

rules.rule: The verb carries the action — it is not a placeholder for a nominalization.
rules.rationale: When the real action is buried in a noun — decision, analysis, consideration — the verb becomes meaningless filler. The action belongs in the verb.
rules.example_incorrect: The team made a quick decision.
rules.note: See nominalization_blocklist.json for full list of verb-noun constructions to replace.

rules.rule: Do not bury the main verb after a long introductory phrase.
rules.rationale: When the verb arrives late in the sentence, the reader must hold incomplete meaning while waiting for it. Lead with subject and verb, then add context.
rules.example_correct: The pipeline failed at 3am, triggering an automatic shutdown.
rules.example_incorrect: At 3am on Tuesday, following a pressure spike detected by the monitoring system, the pipeline failed.
rules.fix: Restructure so subject and verb appear in the first half of the sentence.

rules.rule: Avoid split constructions that place modifiers between auxiliary and main verb unnecessarily.
rules.rationale: Modifiers between auxiliary and main verb slow the reader without adding clarity.
rules.example_correct: The team had already reviewed the report.
rules.example_incorrect: The team had, in the course of the previous afternoon, reviewed the report.

preferred_verb_types.description: In order of preference for DSCL output
order: 1. Strong transitive verbs — subject acts on object directly (destroy, close, send, block, confirm)
2. Strong intransitive verbs — subject acts without object (fall, stop, fail, collapse, move)
3. Linking verbs with specific predicates — subject is described precisely (the road remains closed)
4. Auxiliary + main verb — compound tenses used only when tense requires it
5. Forms of to be — last resort, only when no action verb is possible
